info.name: Triage98 API
info.description:
Complete API collection for Triage98 (local-first operations triage).

Covers the currently implemented surface: meta/health, auth (register, login, me), and workspaces (create, list, detail, delete, invites, members, departments), plus negative tests for every access-control rule.

Usage:
1. Import this collection AND `triage98.postman_environment.json`, then select the "Triage98 (local)" environment (top-right).
2. Start the API: `npm run dev:server` (defaults to http://localhost:4000).
3. Run the whole collection top to bottom with the Collection Runner. The requests are ordered as a story: an owner registers, creates a workspace, mints an invite; a second user registers, joins, and leaves; the owner then deletes the workspace.

Auth: the collection sends `Authorization: Bearer {{token}}` (the owner) by default. Requests acting as the second user override this to `{{memberToken}}`; anonymous requests override to "No Auth".

request.description: Service banner. Returns { name, status }.
Liveness + dependency probe. 200 with status 'ok' when Postgres and Redis are up; 503 'degraded' when a dependency is down (never hangs).

exec: // Generate a unique email each run so re-running never 409s.

request.description: Create an account. Returns { token, user }. Rate-limited to 5/hour/IP.
Verify credentials, return a new session token. Uses the email captured by Register. Rate-limited to 10/min/IP.
Current user from the Bearer token (inherited from the collection).

exec: pm.test('generic error message', () => pm.expect(body.error.message).to.eql('Invalid email or password.'));

request.description: Wrong password returns the same generic 401 as an unknown email (no user enumeration).
Without a Bearer token the endpoint returns 401.
Invalid body returns 400 with per-field messages under error.details.

item.name: Workspaces
item.description: Workspaces, invites, members, and departments. Ordered as a runnable story — run top to bottom.

exec: // Unique name per run so the slug never collides with a previous run.
pm.test('slug is derived from the name', () => pm.expect(w.slug).to.match(/^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$/));

request.description: Create a workspace and become its owner. Seeds the six default departments. Capped at 3 owned workspaces per user; throttled 5/hour/user.
Every workspace the caller belongs to, with their role in each.

exec: pm.test('returns the workspace and the caller role', () => {

request.description: Workspace detail plus the caller's own role. Requires membership.
Departments in the workspace. Any member may read these — requesters need them to file.

exec: pm.test('is not flagged as a default', () => pm.expect(department.isDefault).to.be.false);

request.description: Add a routing target. Admin or owner only. Capped at 10 custom departments.
Rename or re-describe a department. At least one field is required.

exec: pm.test('explains what is missing', () => pm.expect(pm.response.json().error.message).to.include('Provide a name or a description'));

request.description: A PATCH with nothing to change is a client bug, not a silent no-op.
The six built-in departments cannot be deleted — triage always needs somewhere to route to.
Delete a custom department. Rejected with 409 if tickets still reference it.
Mint an invite code. Admin or owner; only an owner may invite an admin. Set expiresInDays to 0 for a code that never expires. Capped at 5 active invites.
Admin or owner only — requesters have no business seeing invite codes.
A second account, so the invite and member rules can be exercised for real. Register is throttled 5/hour/IP — if this 429s, wait or restart Redis.
Redeem an invite code as the second user. Accepts a bare code or a pasted invite link (…/join?code=…). Single-use codes are burned here; this one is reusable.
Redeeming a code for a workspace you already belong to is a no-op that does not consume the invite.

exec: pm.test('owner and the new member are both listed', () => pm.expect(members).to.have.lengthOf(2));

request.description: Any member may see who else is in the workspace.
Owner-only. An admin cannot change roles, and nobody can change their own.
Now an admin, the second user still cannot change roles — theirs or anyone else's.
Give up your own membership. Owners cannot leave — they delete the workspace instead.
A non-member gets the same 404 as a nonexistent slug, so slugs cannot be probed for existence.
Letting the owner walk out would strand the workspace with nobody able to administer it.
Kill a code while keeping the record. Owners may revoke any invite; admins only their own.
Unknown, expired, revoked, and already-used codes all return one message — an invite code is a bearer credential.

exec: pm.test('asks for a valid code', () => pm.expect(pm.response.json().error.message).to.include('valid invite code'));

request.description: Input that cannot be an invite code is rejected before any lookup.
Every workspace route sits behind requireAuth.
A slug nobody owns is indistinguishable from one you are not a member of.

exec: pm.test('403 means the read-only lock fired; 404 means you have not joined demo-clinic', () => pm.expect(pm.response.json().error.code).to.be.oneOf(['forbidden', 'not_found']));

request.description: The seeded demo workspace accepts reads but rejects every mutation, whatever role you hold in it. Join it first with the code DEMO-CLINIC-2026 to see the 403 rather than a 404.
Deleting cascades to every request, ticket, and note, so the caller retypes the slug.
Owner only, with the slug as confirmation. Cleans up everything this run created.
